content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

We’ve all written code like this.
You inject a dependency that may or may not exist, then every time you use it, you remember to add another ?. or if statement.
this.auditClient?.record(action);A few files later
if (this.auditClient) {
this.auditClient.record(action);
}Then again
this.auditClient?.record(userEvent);Nothing is technically wrong with this code.
But after hundreds of calls, those null checks become repetitive noise that distracts from your actual business logic.
Today, I’ll show you a cleaner Angular approach using the Null Object Pattern, where consumers never need to know whether the real implementation exists.
The Problem
Imagine your application records audit events.
Sometimes auditing is enabled.
Sometimes it isn’t.
A common implementation looks like this:
export interface AuditRecorder {
record(event: string): void;
}export const AUDIT_RECORDER =
new…
